channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: ‘The Maids,’ With Yerin Ha, Asks: Has Life Become One Big Performance?
item.description: In his rethinking of Jean Genet’s classic work about class and power, Kip Williams ponders “a world that gives you every opportunity not to be yourself.”
item.dc:creator: Roslyn Sulcas
item.media:credit: Richard Termine for The New York Times
item.media:description: Power dynamic: Saban and Ha in Kip Williams’s production, which was rewritten in a contemporary idiom while hewing closely to Genet’s plot.

item.title: Opera Philadelphia: Behind Its Comeback
item.description: Anthony Roth Costanzo took over Opera Philadelphia when it was “three weeks away from stopping payroll.” Now, it is rebounding with a surplus.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:credit: Hannah Yoon for The New York Times

item.title: Celebrity Assistants Exist to Indulge Their Bosses, but When Does Duty Cross a Line?
item.description: Matthew Perry’s assistant injected the ketamine that killed his employer. His sentencing has some in the demanding profession considering the power dynamics involved.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: Raymond Hall/GC Images, via Getty Images
item.media:description: An autopsy report attributed Matthew Perry’s death in 2023 to the “acute effects" of ketamine. The actor’s personal assistant had found him unresponsive in a hot tub in his home in Los Angeles.

item.title: L.A. Philharmonic Names Daniel Harding as Music Director
item.description: The Philharmonic, taking a more traditional route than in the past, has named the British-born conductor Daniel Harding as its next music director.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:credit: Benjamin Malapris for The New York Times
item.media:description: The conductor Daniel Harding, who will begin as the music director of the Los Angeles Philharmonic in the 2027-28 season.

item.title: ‘Chess’ Will Close With Lea Michele’s Departure, in a Change of Plans
item.description: The Broadway musical had planned to keep going, but box-office sales made clear Michele’s appeal was essential. It will now close June 21.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: Lea Michele in the revival of “Chess” now running at Broadway’s Imperial Theater.

item.title: ‘Armageddon’: The Blockbuster Melodrama That Left Our Heads Spinning
item.description: Michael Bay wasn’t remotely worried about the science in his 1998 science-fiction action hit that launched an elite ensemble cast to space.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam

item.title: Sonny Rollins: 12 Essential Albums
item.description: The towering saxophonist, who died at 95, was a master of living in the moment. Listen to some of his most compelling work, onstage and in the studio.
item.dc:creator: Hank Shteamer
item.media:description: Sonny Rollins performing in Nice, France, in 2009.

item.title: Julia Louis-Dreyfus to Star in ‘Other Desert Cities’ on Broadway
item.description: The revival, also featuring Ed Harris, Allison Janney, Lily Rabe and Joe Keery, will begin performances in September at the Hudson Theater.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: Louis-Dreyfus, photographed earlier this month at the Cannes film festival, will be making her Broadway debut in the play.

item.title: 5 Great Songs I Encountered This Week
item.description: Hear Mick Taylor’s swan song, Grace Ives’s exuberant crushing and a foundational hip-hop sample.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:credit: J. Wilds/Hulton Archive, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The Rolling Stones in 1969: From left, Charlie Watts, Mick Taylor, Mick Jagger, Keith Richards and Bill Wyman.

item.title: Sonny Rollins, Giant of the Jazz Saxophone, Is Dead at 95
item.description: Even by the standards of a music that prizes individuality, he stood out, as both a musician and a personality.
item.dc:creator: Peter Keepnews
item.media:credit: Stephanie Berger for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sonny Rollins in 2006. He flirted with the avant-garde, jazz-rock fusion and other styles over the years, but he was ultimately unclassifiable.

item.title: Lizzo Addresses the Backlash and Performs Live at The New York Times
item.description: During a special edition of “Popcast,” the singer discussed her new album and the public fallout from a series of lawsuits. Then she performed a stripped-down set.
item.dc:creator: Joe Coscarelli and Jon Caramanica
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times

item.title: Ryan Speedo Green in ‘Die Walküre’: Preview of the Met Opera
item.description: Ryan Speedo Green has been previewing his take on Wotan, ruler of the gods, with Gustavo Dudamel and the Los Angeles Philharmonic.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:description: Ryan Speedo Green, front, as Wotan in “Die Walküre,” near Christine Goerke as Brünnhilde.

item.title: All Quiet at the Kennedy Center
item.description: David Jones has been the principal clarinetist of the Kennedy Center’s orchestra for five presidents. A cascade of cancellations means he hasn’t played there since February.
item.dc:creator: Michaela Towfighi
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Jared Soares for The New York Times
item.media:description: The dozens of musicians in the Kennedy Center Opera House Orchestra face an uncertain future. “It felt like we were just punched in the chest,” David Jones said.

item.title: How Art Auctions Choreographed a $2.5 Billion Comeback
item.description: After four years of uneven sales, the auction houses engineered a successful season by redefining the expectations of buyers and sellers—and avoiding flops.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small, Julia Halperin and Tim F. Schneider
item.media:credit: Pollock-Krasner Foundation/Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York; Photo by George Etheredge for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jackson Pollock, “Number 7A” (1948), a horizontal drip painting, an example of the artist’s “action painting” style from a peak period, is a highlight of the Newhouse collection sale at Christie’s that fetched $181.2 million including fees.

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Slams Trump for Missing His Son’s Wedding
item.description: “It was a small ceremony with about 40 guests, none of whom were his father,” the late-night host said of Donald Trump Jr.’s wedding in the Bahamas over the weekend.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix

item.title: 4 Takeaways From the ‘Summer House’ Season 10 Reunion
item.description: The highly anticipated episode delved into the timeline of the relationship between Amanda Batula and West Wilson and the pair’s confrontation with Ciara Miller and the rest of their housemates.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:description: Kyle Cooke and Ciara Miller on the “Summer House” Season 10 reunion.

item.title: How Cannes Is Grappling With Changes
item.description: Reporting from the Cannes Film Festival, our film critic Alissa Wilkinson describes how the event is both fending off and embracing aspects of artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson and Edward Vega

item.title: John Barbour, Who Played Key Role in the Rise of Reality TV, Dies at 93
item.description: He was a creator and producer of “Real People,” a hit NBC series about eccentrics and unusual lifestyles, which spawned imitators like “That’s Incredible!”
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:description: John Barbour in 1981. Mr. Barbour was a producer and writer of the good-natured series “Real People,” bantering cheerfully about its subjects with the members of an onscreen panel.

item.title: Ben Morea, Principal Provocateur of 1960s Anarchist Group, Dies at 84
item.description: An abstract painter who wanted to creatively destroy the gap between art and revolution, he loosely led a band of radicals with a profane name.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Rosenwald
item.media:description: Ben Morea in 2016 at an exhibition of his paintings in New York City. He was mostly known for an anarchist-centered activism he called “armed love.”

item.title: ‘Spider-Noir’ Is a Multicolor Spin on ‘Spider-Man’
item.description: The new Spider-series stars Nicolas Cage as a hard-boiled investigator and former web slinger. It comes in both black-and-white and color versions.
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman
item.media:description: “Spider-Noir,” with Nicolas Cage, channels modern comic books and pulpy Hollywood films of the past.

item.title: The International Dance League Wants to Be the ‘NBA of Dance’
item.description: The International Dance League wants to give dancers a career path and to create stars. “Commercialization is good,” said a founder of the league.
item.dc:creator: Margaret Fuhrer and Shuran Huang
item.media:description: GRV, from Los Angeles, is another of the league’s six founding teams.

item.title: Broadway’s ‘The Lost Boys’ and Its Flying Vampire Spectacle
item.description: The Broadway production of “The Lost Boys” takes full advantage of the Palace Theater’s abundant height and depth, making the show feel like “a massive trust fall.”
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson and Sara Krulwich
item.media:description: Multiple actors take flight at the same time in the spectacle-heavy production, which requires a lot of backstage work to ensure nothing, and no one, collides.

item.title: Traveling the Hudson River Valley With Art as a Guide
item.description: Artists like Thomas Cole and Frederic Church left us evidence of what America’s landscape was like 200 years ago. Using science, and art history, a writer follows their trail through the Hudson River Valley to better understand how our world has changed.
item.dc:creator: Kim Beil and Tony Cenicola
item.media:description: An aerial view of Olana, Frederic Church’s home in the Hudson Valley. The estate inspired the preservation of nearly 3,000 acres of land within its viewshed.

item.title: In ‘For All Mankind,’ America Wins by Losing
item.description: The current season of this space drama is set largely on Mars. The creators say this alternate world could have been possible if the nation had stayed focused on the stars.
item.dc:creator: Conor Dougherty

item.title: ‘Star City,’ and 5 More Things to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: A spinoff of “For All Mankind” airs on Apple TV, and a new Netflix series tells the incredible story of Brazil’s win at the 1970 World Cup.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:description: “Star City” expands the story lines of the series “For All Mankind,” this time from the perspective of the Soviet Union.

item.title: ‘Mandalorian and Grogu’ Tops Box Office as Disney Bets on ‘Star Wars’ Revival
item.description: The movie, which cost $300 million to make and market, was expected to collect about $102 million from Thursday through Monday at domestic theaters.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.category: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu (Movie)
item.media:credit: Andres Kudacki for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu” was intended to sell toys as much as movie tickets.

item.title: Bonnaroo, Governors Ball and 7 More Summer Music Festivals Not to Miss
item.description: Can’t get to Lollapalooza or Outside Lands this year? Want to see Lizzo play flute with the Chicago Symphony Orchestra? Here are some options.
item.dc:creator: Danielle Dowling
item.media:description: The Ferris wheel at the Bonnaroo Musical and Arts Festival in Manchester, Tenn., last year. The long-running festival will return in June, along with six stages featuring bands such as the Strokes and Geese.

item.title: Alamo Drafthouse Instituted Mobile Ordering. Super Fans Are Not Pleased.
item.description: Known for its strict no-phones policy, the Alamo Drafthouse has instituted mobile ordering for its in-seat meals, upsetting some of its biggest fans.
item.dc:creator: Brendan Klinkenberg
item.media:description: Moviegoers at an Alamo Drafthouse in Austin, Texas. It has long served food to patrons at their seats, but now they order by phone instead of writing out their requests.

item.title: How This Summer’s Movies Shift the Connection With Their Related Toys
item.description: “The Mandalorian and Grogu,” “Masters of the Universe” and “Toy Story 5” hail from franchises that are shifting the connection with related products.
item.dc:creator: Robert Ito
item.category: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu (Movie)
item.media:description: A fan carrying a Grogu doll, one of many products being used to market “Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu.”

item.title: Nick Kroll Keeps It Raunchy and Animated with “Mating Season”
item.description: With “Mating Season,” Nick Kroll continues to lead the pack when it comes to the booming genre of cartoons that revel in their raciness.
item.dc:creator: Maya Phillips and Sela Shiloni
item.media:credit: Sela Shiloni for The New York Times

item.title: Director Cristian Mungiu’s ‘Fjord’ Wins Palme d’Or at Cannes Film Festival
item.description: The film, from the Romanian director Cristian Mungiu, explores the tension between religious conservatism and social liberalism. The award closed a festival that felt comparatively muted to many.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis
item.media:description: The director Cristian Mungiu, center, accepted the Palme d’Or in Cannes, France, on Saturday.

item.title: Grizz Chapman, Actor Known for His Role on ’30 Rock,’ Dies at 52
item.description: Mr. Chapman, who played a member of Tracy Morgan’s entourage on the show, died on Friday, according to his cousin.
item.dc:creator: Aimee Ortiz
item.media:description: Grizzwald Chapman in 2012 in New York City.

item.title: Colbert’s Sudden Return to Late Night? It Happened on Public Access.
item.description: A day after the comedian hosted the final episode of “The Late Show With Stephen Colbert” on CBS, he hosted “Only in Monroe,” a community-access program in Monroe, Mich.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: Husband of Prominent New York Gallerist Convicted in Murder-for-Hire Plot
item.description: Brent Sikkema, who championed artists like Kara Walker, was found stabbed to death in his apartment in Brazil in 2024 amid divorce proceedings with his spouse.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.media:description: The entrance to the apartment in Rio de Janeiro where the art dealer Brent Sikkema was reported to be found stabbed to death in 2024.

item.title: Letting Her Art (and Birds) Do the Talking
item.description: Lynette Yiadom-Boakye prefers to remain unseen, but her intriguing new canvases, drawings and writings speak volumes.
item.dc:creator: Aruna D’Souza
item.media:credit: Elliott Jerome Brown Jr. for The New York Times
item.media:description: The artist Lynette Yiadom-Boakye, ahead of her exhibition, “Many A Moonlit Caveat,” at Jack Shainman Gallery in Chelsea, with her painting, “9 pm Normandy.”

item.title: As Draco Malfoy on Broadway, Tom Felton Draws a Crowd
item.description: Fans are traveling great distances for the chance to meet Tom Felton, who has revived a now grown-up Draco Malfoy on Broadway in “Harry Potter and The Cursed Child.”
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr and Vidhya Nagarajan
item.category: Harry Potter and the Cursed Child (Play)

item.title: Shooting Stars at the amfAR Cannes Gala
item.description: Our photographer took in the art and fashion on display as guests gathered in seaside Antibes, France, to raise money for AIDS research.
item.dc:creator: Landon Nordeman
item.category: amfAR, The Foundation for AIDS Research
item.media:credit: Landon Nordeman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Coco Rocha at the amfAR Cannes gala in Cap d’Antibes, France, on Thursday.

item.title: Which Cannes Films Could Become Oscar Contenders?
item.description: The American “Club Kid” and Spanish “La Bola Negra” are among the most likely to impress academy voters, while Scarlett Johansson and Javier Bardem also stood out.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan
item.media:description: Jordan Firstman, center, in his directorial debut, “Club Kid,” which sold to A24 for a reported $17 million.

item.title: John Marion, the ‘God of Auctioneers in America,’ Dies at 92
item.description: For three decades, he was the face of the auction house Sotheby’s, presiding over an era in which art evolved from an object of passion into a commodity.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green
item.media:credit: McCabe/Daily Express, via Hulton Archive, via Getty Images
item.media:description: John Marion in 1965. “He was the god of auctioneers in America,” a colleague said. “He could make people bid by sheer force of his personality.”

item.title: Olivia Rodrigo, Vince Staples: 8 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: Olivia Rodrigo’s orchestral angst, Charli XCX’s fashion crisis and Vince Staples’s frustrated sing-rap.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles

item.title: He’s Trolling Your Trash, and Turning It Into Art
item.description: Thomas Dambo built a global following by turning Denmark’s scrap into giant, hidden forest creatures. Now, the art world is finally letting him inside.
item.dc:creator: Alice Godwin
item.media:credit: Hilary Swift for The New York Times
item.media:description: Trolls under construction in Dambo’s studio. The heads take two or three weeks to make, an assistant said.

item.title: This Is How You Steal a Scene Using Only a Bank
item.description: Deposit slips, vintage ledgers and more: David Korins’s Tony-nominated, 27,000-pound set for “Dog Day Afternoon” is as much a technical feat as a 1970s throwback.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik and Amir Hamja
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times

item.title: Travel Through the Looking Glass in Yoshi and the Mysterious Book
item.description: This year’s new Nintendo Switch 2 games include a wonderful adventure for Yoshi, magic tennis rackets for Baby Mario and Baby Luigi, and a Sims-Animal Crossing mashup.
item.dc:creator: Harold Goldberg
item.category: Yoshi and the Mysterious Book (Video Game)

item.title: Nicole Polizzi of ‘Jersey Shore’ Relaxes by Watching Zombies
item.description: “My favorite horror movies are with zombies and watching the end of the world happen and people having to live in this new world of zombies.”
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez

item.title: Boots Riley Takes on Fast Fashion in a Surreal Comedy
item.description: Alissa Wilkinson, a New York Times film critic, reviews the very political “I Love Boosters.”
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson, Haimy Assefa, Edward Vega and Laura Salaberry

item.title: Rob Base, Rapper Known for ‘It Takes Two,’ Dies at 59
item.description: The singer died on Friday after a “private battle with cancer,” according to his official Instagram account.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk
item.media:description: The rapper Rob Base in 2022. He was known for the hit song “It Takes Two.”

item.title: Free Theater in NYC: 15 Live Shows to See This Spring and Summer
item.description: Chekhov, Broadway stars and, yes, Shakespeare. There’s no shortage of free productions in the city this time of year. Here are 15 shows worth seeing.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Sherman
item.media:description: The cast of “Romeo & Juliet,” a new Shakespeare in the Park production, at the Delacorte Theater in Central Park.

item.title: Five Horror Movies to Stream Now
item.description: Evil this month looks just plain weird, from a despairing children’s TV show to a demonic buffet.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg
item.category: The House Was Not Hungry Then (Movie)

item.title: Stephen Colbert’s Last ‘Late Show’: Laughing Well Is the Best Revenge
item.description: The “Late Show” cancellation was a disappointment. But a surreally lovely final episode turned it into a cancellebration.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
item.media:description: Stephen Colbert and an array of famous friends gave “The Late Show” a joyous send-off on Thursday night.

item.title: James Gray Stands Out at a Disappointing Cannes Film Festival
item.description: James Gray’s “Paper Tiger” is among the stronger titles, which also include Ryusuke Hamaguchi’s “All of a Sudden” and Pawel Pawlikowski’s “Fatherland.”
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis
item.media:description: Adam Driver is among the stars of “Paper Tiger,” directed by James Gray.

item.title: Billy Eichner on ‘Bros’ and His New Memoir
item.description: The comedian needed time to process the poor turnout for his gay rom-com, “Bros.” His vulnerable new memoir may surprise “Billy on the Street” fans.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman
item.media:credit: Brian Karlsson for The New York Times

item.title: 7 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu (Movie)
Tom Clancy's Jack Ryan: Ghost War (Movie)

item.title: Watch Scorsese Make a Mean Sandwich in ‘The Mandalorian and Grogu’
item.description: The director Jon Favreau narrates a sequence featuring a character who works at a food truck and is voiced by Martin Scorsese.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy
item.category: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu (Movie)

item.title: ‘Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu’: What to Know Before Seeing the Movie
item.description: It draws from the Disney+ series. Here’s a reminder of the events of the show and how those fit into the larger franchise.
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray
item.category: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu (Movie)
item.media:description: Pedro Pascal as the Mandalorian and his little friend in a scene from the new movie.

item.title: ‘The Yogurt Shop Murders’: When a Documentary Becomes Part of the Story
item.description: Weeks after an HBO docuseries on the 1991 murders of four girls aired last year, their killer was identified, and the race was on to add a new final chapter.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam

item.title: ‘Girl, Interrupted’ Continues, This Time Onstage at the Public Theater
item.description: This 1993 memoir, which became a film with Winona Ryder and Angelina Jolie, is now a play with songs by Aimee Mann. Here’s how the latest iteration came to be.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:description: From left, Sally Shaw, Mia Pak, Juliana Canfield, Katherine Reis, King Princess and Ta’Rea Campbell in “Girl, Interrupted” at the Public Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: In ‘Ask E. Jean,’ a Trump Adversary Examines Her Decision to Speak Out
item.description: E. Jean Carroll won two court cases against him, but it took decades for her to come forward. The film is at its best on the reasons for her hesitance.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: In Defense of the Institution of Late Night
item.description: “The Late Show With Stephen Colbert” has ended. Our culture critic Jason Zinoman describes what we stand to lose.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman, Haimy Assefa, Gabriel Blanco and Laura Salaberry
item.category: Late Show With David Letterman (TV Program)

item.title: What to Read This Summer: NYT Reading Bucket List
item.description: Read along with the Book Review this summer: Can you check off five items before fall arrives? (This year, there are prizes!)
item.dc:creator: The New York Times Books Staff

item.title: Superman Experience at Warner Bros. Mirrors Hollywood’s Change
item.description: Inside the reworked Stage 5, a hallowed soundstage where John Wayne once roamed and the cast of “Friends” sipped coffee on couches.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: ‘The Mandalorian and Grogu’ | Anatomy of a Scene
item.description: The director Jon Favreau narrates a sequence from his film, featuring a character voiced by Martin Scorsese.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy
item.category: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu (Movie)

item.title: Stephen Colbert’s Last ‘Late Show’ Features Famous Friends and Paul McCartney
item.description: Colbert was joined by famous friends, fellow hosts and special guests like Paul McCartney for the finale of “The Late Show.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
item.media:description: Stephen Colbert’s final “Late Show” included celebrity guests, musical performances and an absurdist final bit.

item.title: ‘Ladies First’ Review: A Nightmare for Men
item.description: Sacha Baron Cohen and Rosamund Pike star in this role-reversal workplace comedy where women rule the world.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Kennedy
